preamble: The phonetic transformation from the voiceless alveolo-palatal affricate /tʃ/ to the voiceless velar plosive /k/ is generally considered an uncommon or unattested regular historical sound change in many linguistic frameworks. The inverse process, palatalization (/k/ to /tʃ/), is far more prevalent. However, specific instances of this depalatalization and deaffrication have been documented in certain language families.
phoneticEffects: This shift involves a depalatalization, where the point of articulation moves backward from the alveolo-palatal region to the velar region

examples.note: The Chontal and Tzotzil name 'Chak' (thunder) corresponds to Yukatekan 'Kawak'.
The Chontal and Tzotzil name 'Chak' (thunder) corresponds to K'ichean 'Kawuq'.

sources: Tedlock, D. (2009). 2000 Years of Mayan Literature. University of California Press.
Hickey, R. (2003). Techniques of historical linguistics. Springer.
Garrett, A., & Johnson, K. (2013). Diachronic evidence for the synchronic grammar: The case of long-distance liquid. Rutgers Optimality Archive.
